explicit_text: Hell's Kitchen is where I played my last public jazz gig on March 11, right before everything shut down due to the pandemic. It was a great gig with Phil, and it felt like the last moment of normalcy before the venues closed. That place was part of my regular gig calendar, and losing those live venues was a huge blow to musicians like me. It’s a key location in my story of how the pandemic abruptly changed my performance life.

explicit_text: The Lexington in Saint Paul was one of my regular gig spots before the pandemic hit. I used to play there frequently as part of my busy schedule, which could be five to eight gigs a week. When the pandemic forced venues to close, The Lexington was one of many places that shut down, which meant a sudden stop to my live performances. It’s part of the network of venues that supported my career in the Twin Cities area.

explicit_text: Cadenza is the music school where I taught many students before the pandemic forced us to move online. The owner, Dan, is a good friend, and we worked together to transition lessons to a virtual format. It was challenging at first, especially with the tech side, but I managed to keep teaching my students there remotely. Cadenza represents a community and support system that helped me continue my work during this difficult time.

explicit_text: Amazon was crucial for me during the pandemic because I ordered a tech interface from them to improve my online teaching setup. They delivered it quickly, which helped me get my lessons running more smoothly after a rough start. Without Amazon’s fast shipping, I might have struggled longer with the technical problems of remote teaching. It’s an example of how technology and services helped musicians adapt during the crisis.

explicit_text: Pete is someone I know who was at Macalester College, and I believe he was an adjunct professor there. His experience reflects the challenges faced by many educators who had to quickly acquire new tech skills without much institutional support. Pete’s situation helped me think about the broader responsibilities placed on teachers in this crisis.
